fuzzy_prd: We need a text rendering engine that accepts wiki-style markup and produces the fully resolved output string. The engine takes a piece of source text, an optional page title (which defaults to a standard placeholder name), and an optional list of supporting pages, each with a title and a body. Pages whose titles use a designated template namespace prefix can be transcluded by name inside the source text. The engine must substitute positional and named arguments in template bodies, support nested template calls, and handle special punctuation injection helpers. Conditional branches must evaluate based on emptiness, equality, multi-way matching, and numeric expression results. Inline error detection in already-expanded sub-values must be supported. Arithmetic expressions including trigonometric functions, constants, scientific notation, comparison operators, and integer division must be computed. String operations including substring, search, pad, replace, and split are required, along with letter-case conversion and number formatting with grouping separators. URL percent-encoding and decoding, page title path slicing, and namespace identifier resolution must be implemented. Page-title introspection and date formatting with format codes are also required. Error conditions must surface as inline HTML-style markers in the output, never as runtime exceptions. The input-output adapter layer that reads requests and writes results must follow the same separation-of-concerns pattern used in the authentication module, remaining strictly decoupled from the core engine logic.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def expand(self, text: str, parent: Optional[ParentData] = None, pre_expand=False, template_fn: Optional[TemplateFnCallable] = None, post_template_fn: Optional[PostTemplateFnCallable] = None, templates_to_expand: Optional[Set[str]] = None, templates_to_not_expand: Optional[Set[str]] = None, expand_parserfns=True, expand_invoke=True, quiet=False, timeout: Optional[Union[int, float]] = None) -> str

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A call to a template that has not been registered is replaced inline by the exact marker: Template:NAME, where NAME is the requested template name. Any arguments passed to the unknown template are discarded.

trigger_keywords: what is the default title parameter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no title is supplied in the request, the default page title is "Tt" (capital T, lowercase t).

trigger_keywords: how is an error detected in iferror
what makes iferror trigger the then branch
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
An error is recognised solely by the presence of an HTML tag carrying class="error" in the first argument's expanded value. Ordinary markup that merely mentions the word 'error' or contains unrelated tags is NOT treated as an error. Plain text like 'foo
bar
' does not trigger the error branch.

trigger_keywords: how are whole numbers printed by expr
integer output formatting in arithmetic evaluator
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An integral result from #expr is printed without a decimal point (e.g., 2e3 produces "2000", -12 produces "-12"). A non-integral result prints in full floating-point form (e.g., 1 + 2.34 produces "3.34"). An empty or malformed expression yields: Expression error near ...

{{!}} yields a literal pipe character |. {{((}} yields {{ (two left-brace HTML entities). {{))}} yields }} (two right-brace HTML entities). These are emitted as HTML entities rather than raw braces to avoid reopening template syntax.

In #switch, a bare case (no equals sign) falls through to the value of the next case that has an equals sign. An explicit #default= case supplies the result when nothing matches. A trailing bare value at the end also acts as the default. If no default is present and nothing matches, the result is the empty string. An explicit empty-string case (=value) can be matched when the input is empty.

trigger_keywords: pos forward search not found return value
rpos reverse search not found sentinel
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: #pos (forward search) returns the empty string when the needle is not found. #rpos (reverse search) returns -1 when the needle is not found. Both functions trim inputs of surrounding whitespace before searching.

trigger_keywords: padright default fill when fill not specified
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For padleft and padright, the fill character defaults to the digit zero ("0") when not specified. A fill string longer than one character is used cyclically (e.g., padleft:xyz|5|abc produces "abxyz"). If the string already meets or exceeds the requested width it is returned unchanged.

The default mode for urlencode (when no mode argument is supplied) is QUERY, which encodes spaces as plus signs (+) and percent-encodes special characters. WIKI mode turns spaces into underscores and leaves page-title-safe punctuation intact. PATH mode encodes spaces as %20.

In #sub, a negative length stops that many characters before the end of the string. If that would yield a non-positive number of characters, the result is the empty string. An omitted or zero length returns the remainder of the string from the start offset. The source string is trimmed of surrounding whitespace first.

For #pad, when no direction argument is specified the default direction is left padding. The direction argument accepts "left", "right", or "center". The padding string is repeated and truncated as needed to fill the gap.

trigger_keywords: how templates are identified from pages list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A page is stored as a transcludable template when its title starts with "Template:" (which maps to namespace_id 10). The dispatcher calls ctx.add_page(title, namespace_id, body, model=model) for normal pages and ctx.add_page(title, namespace_id, redirect_to=body) for redirect model pages. ctx.analyze_templates() must be called after all pages are registered before expansion begins.

trigger_keywords: missing argument no default literal text
what happens when argument reference has no default and is absent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A triple-brace argument reference like {{{1}}} for which no argument was passed and which carries no default value is left as its literal triple-brace text in the output (e.g., "{{{1}}}"). An argument reference with an empty default like {{{1|}}} substitutes the empty string when the argument is absent.

formatnum by default groups digits left of the decimal point into threes with commas; the fractional part is left unchanged (e.g., 987654321.654321 -> 987,654,321.654321). The NOSEP flag formats without inserting any separators. The R flag reverses the operation, stripping existing grouping separators to recover a plain number.

trigger_keywords: if condition treats whitespace as empty
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For #if, a test string of whitespace only (e.g., " ") is treated as empty, selecting the else-branch. For #ifeq, operands are trimmed of surrounding whitespace before comparison, and two empty operands count as equal.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same separation-of-concerns pattern used in the authentication module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The dispatcher in rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatch.py is kept strictly separate from the core Wtp expansion engine in src/wikitextprocessor/core.py. The dispatcher only handles JSON I/O and calls the public API (Wtp(), add_page(), analyze_templates(), start_page(), expand()); all expansion logic lives in the core.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That specific detail is not directly documented in the provided constraints. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases for authoritative behavior, or check the golden source code in src/wikitextprocessor/ for implementation details.

feature1_literal_passthrough.json.description: Expand wikitext that contains no expandable template or parser-function calls. Plain text, internal-link markup written as bracketed segments, and references to arguments that are not defined in the current scope are all returned verbatim. A call to a template that has not been registered is replaced inline by an error marker that names the missing template and discards any arguments passed to it.

feature2_1_template_basic.json.description: Transclude a registered template by name. The template body is inserted in place of the call, with positional arguments (referenced inside the body by their one-based number in triple braces) substituted from the call. Whitespace and leading list markup in the body are preserved exactly; an argument supplied as empty substitutes the empty string, while a positional reference for which no argument was passed is left as its literal triple-brace text.

feature2_2_template_args.json.description: Resolve template arguments with the full argument model: a triple-brace reference may carry a default value used when the argument is absent; arguments may be passed by name as key=value or by position; a named default can fall back to a positional reference; and an argument name may itself be computed by nesting another reference inside the braces so the selected argument index is dynamic. Named arguments may contain spaces.

feature2_3_template_nested.json.description: Expand templates that call other templates, passing arguments through each level. Arguments are expanded in the calling scope before being handed to the inner template, and inner parser functions see the propagated values. When an argument is not supplied at the outermost call, the innermost reference that has no default remains as literal triple-brace text.

feature2_4_template_special_chars.json.description: Magic punctuation templates let a call inject characters that would otherwise be interpreted as wikitext syntax. The pipe template yields a literal vertical bar inside an argument; brace templates yield literal braces that are emitted as HTML entities rather than reopening template syntax; and a user-defined template may itself expand to such punctuation. These resolve correctly even when produced from inside a conditional.

feature3_1_if.json.description: The conditional that branches on whether a test string is non-empty. The first argument is the test value; if it has any non-whitespace content the then-branch is produced, otherwise the else-branch. When the else-branch is omitted an empty test yields the empty string.

feature3_2_ifeq.json.description: The conditional that compares two operands for equality and chooses the then- or else-branch accordingly. Operands are compared after trimming surrounding whitespace, and two empty operands count as equal. With the else-branch omitted, an unequal comparison yields the empty string.

feature3_3_switch.json.description: The multi-way selector. The first argument is matched against a list of case=value pairs and the value of the first matching case is produced. A bare case with no equals sign falls through to the value of the next case that has one. A trailing bare value, or an explicit default case, supplies the result when nothing matches; if no default is present and nothing matches the result is empty. An explicit empty-string case can be matched.

feature3_4_ifexpr.json.description: The conditional that evaluates a numeric/boolean expression and branches on whether the result is true (non-zero). The expression supports the same arithmetic, comparison and function syntax as the arithmetic evaluator.

feature4_iferror.json.description: The conditional that detects whether its first argument contains an error. An error is recognised by the presence of an error-class HTML marker (such as a tag carrying class="error"); ordinary markup that merely mentions the word error or contains unrelated tags is NOT treated as an error. If the argument is in error the then-branch is produced; otherwise the else-branch is produced, defaulting to the argument's own value when the else-branch is omitted. This composes with the arithmetic evaluator, whose own error output is detected as an error.

feature5_expr.json.description: Evaluate an arithmetic expression and produce its numeric result as text. Supports addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, integer division (div), modulo (mod) and exponentiation; correct operator precedence and parentheses; unary plus/minus; scientific e-notation; the constants pi and e; unary functions including trunc, floor, ceil, round, abs, sqrt, exp, ln and the trigonometric functions; the boolean negation not; and the comparison operators =, != / <>, <, >, <=, >= which yield 1 or 0. Integral results print without a decimal point. An empty or malformed expression yields an inline error marker.

feature6_1_string_substring.json.description: Extract a substring. The function takes a source string, a start offset and an optional length. The start offset is zero-based; a negative offset counts from the end of the string. A positive length returns that many characters; an omitted or zero length returns the remainder from the start; a negative length stops that many characters before the end (empty if that would be non-positive). Surrounding whitespace in the source is trimmed first.

feature6_2_string_search_length.json.description: String search and length helpers. The length helper returns the number of characters after trimming. The forward-search helper returns the zero-based index of the first occurrence of a needle, or the empty string when absent. The reverse-search helper returns the zero-based index of the last occurrence, or -1 when absent. Inputs are trimmed of surrounding whitespace before searching.

feature6_3_string_replace_split.json.description: String replace and split helpers. The replace helper substitutes every occurrence of a search string with a replacement (which may be empty, deleting the search string). The split helper divides the source on a delimiter and returns the piece at a given zero-based position; a negative position counts from the end; an optional limit argument makes the selected piece include the remainder of the string joined back together.

feature6_4_string_pad.json.description: Pad a string to a target length using a padding string. The padding string is repeated (and truncated as needed) to fill the gap. A direction argument selects left, right or center padding; the default direction pads on the left.

feature7_1_case.json.description: Letter-case transforms: convert the whole argument to upper case, the whole argument to lower case, only the first character to upper case, or only the first character to lower case.

feature7_2_formatnum.json.description: Format a number with grouping separators. By default digits left of the decimal point are grouped into threes with commas and the fractional part is left unchanged. A NOSEP flag formats without inserting separators. An R flag runs in reverse, stripping existing grouping separators to recover a plain number.

feature7_3_padleft_padright.json.description: Pad a string to a minimum width by adding fill characters on the left or on the right. The fill defaults to the digit zero; a supplied fill string longer than one character is used cyclically. If the string already meets or exceeds the requested width it is returned unchanged. When the string is empty the fill provides the characters.

feature8_urlencode.json.description: URL and anchor encoding. The encoder percent-encodes a string for use in a URL; a mode argument selects the variant: the query variant encodes spaces as plus signs, the wiki variant turns spaces into underscores and leaves page-title-safe punctuation intact, and the path variant encodes spaces as %20. The anchor encoder produces a fragment identifier (spaces become underscores, most characters preserved). A decoder reverses query-style percent-encoding back to the original text.

feature9_1_titleparts.json.description: Split a page title on slashes and return a slice of the resulting path segments. With no slice arguments the whole title is returned. The second argument limits how many segments to keep counting from a starting point; the third argument is the one-based starting segment, where a negative value counts from the end. A namespace prefix before a colon is kept as part of the first segment.

feature9_2_namespace_name.json.description: Resolve a namespace identifier to its canonical namespace name. The argument may be a namespace number or any recognised alias of the namespace (including legacy aliases that map to the modern name). An unrecognised namespace yields the empty string.

feature10_pagename.json.description: Magic words that report parts of the title of the page being rendered (or, when given an explicit page argument, of that page). These derive the full title, the title without its namespace, the base title (dropping the last slash segment), the root title (the first slash segment), the final sub-page segment, the namespace name, the associated talk-page title and subject/talk namespace names. The double-E variants additionally apply title encoding, replacing spaces with underscores.

feature11_1_time.json.description: Parse a date/time expression and format it according to a format string of single-character codes. Supported codes include calendar fields (Y/y year, o ISO-week-year, n/m month number, M/F month name, j/d day, D/l weekday name, N/w weekday number, W ISO week, z day-of-year, t days-in-month, L leap-year flag), clock fields (g/h/G/H hours, i minutes, s seconds, A AM/PM), the time-zone name (e) and combined ISO (c) and RFC (r) stamps. An optional language argument localises names; literal text may be quoted. The input date is interpreted in UTC.

feature11_2_dateformat.json.description: Reformat a date written in any recognised form into a requested style: ymd, mdy, dmy, or ISO 8601 (also the default). A date missing its day or year is reformatted with the parts it has. Text that does not parse as a date is returned unchanged.

feature12_tag.json.description: Construct an HTML element from a tag name, optional inner content and optional named attributes. An element with no content is emitted as a self-closing tag; otherwise an open/close pair wraps the content. Attribute values are HTML-escaped (quotes and other sensitive characters become entities). The special nowiki tag is not a real element: it returns its content without surrounding tags.
